title: Introduction to prompt engineering with GitHub Copilot
objectives: Master the 4S method for effective prompts
Understand learning strategies and process flow
Apply best practices for quality suggestions

sections.title: Prompt Engineering Foundations
sections.body:
Prompt engineering is the process of crafting clear instructions to guide AI systems like Copilot to generate context-appropriate code tailored to your project. This ensures code is syntactically, functionally, and contextually correct.

**The 4S Method:**

**Single** — Focus on one task per prompt. Break complex requests into multiple focused prompts.

**Specific** — Use explicit, detailed instructions. Specify requirements, constraints, formats, and edge cases.

**Short** — Keep prompts concise and direct. Each word should add value without overloading the AI.

**Surround** — Use descriptive filenames and keep related files open. This provides rich context for better suggestions.

sections.title: Best Practices and Learning Strategies
sections.body:
**Provide Enough Clarity** — Be explicit in your prompts. Example: "Write a Python function to filter and return even numbers from a given list" is both single-focused and specific.

![alt text](image-1.png)

**Provide Context with Details** — The more contextual information provided, the more fitting the suggestions. Add comments at the top of code to give Copilot more understanding.

![alt text](image-2.png)

**Provide Examples for Learning** — Using examples clarifies requirements and helps Copilot understand patterns quickly, reducing revision cycles.

![alt text](image-3.png)

**Assert and Iterate** — Your first prompt might not yield production-ready code. Enrich your initial comment and prompt again. This iterative approach accelerates development.

**Zero-Shot Learning** — Generate code without examples, relying on foundational training. Ideal for standard patterns.

![alt text](image-4.png)

**One-Shot Learning** — Provide a single example to help Copilot follow your specific patterns and conventions.

![alt text](image-5.png)

**Few-Shot Learning** — Use multiple examples to balance zero-shot unpredictability with fine-tuning precision.

![alt text](image-6.png)

**Chain Prompting** — For complex features, manage long conversations by summarizing context, resetting with focused details, and using concise references.

sections.title: Role Prompting and Process Flow
sections.body:
**Role Prompting** — Act as a specific expert for specialized tasks:
- Security Expert: "Create a password validation function following OWASP guidelines."
- Performance Expert: "Refactor this sorting algorithm for large datasets."
- Testing Specialist: "Create comprehensive unit tests including edge cases."

**Process Flow — Inbound**
1. Secure transmission via HTTPS
2. Context gathering (code, files, project structure)
3. Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) analysis
4. Proxy filtering for malicious requests
5. Content & safety filtering
6. Code generation with LLMs

![alt text](image-7.png)

**Process Flow — Outbound**
7. Post-processing & validation
8. Safety checks (XSS, SQL injection)
9. Delivery of validated suggestions
10. Feedback loop for continuous improvement

![alt text](image-8.png)

sections.title: Data Handling and Context Limits
sections.body:
**Code Suggestions** — Prompts and context are NOT retained for training. Discarded after suggestion is returned. Individual subscribers can opt out of sharing.

**Copilot Chat** — Prompts and responses retained for 28 days outside the code editor. Retention policies may vary in IDE integrations.

**Supported Prompts** — Direct questions, code requests, open-ended queries, and contextual prompts.

**Context Windows** — Standard Copilot: 200-500 lines or few thousand tokens. Copilot Chat: ~4000 tokens (4K context window).

To improve results: Break complex tasks into smaller prompts, provide only relevant snippets, ask focused questions.
bullets: Code suggestions not retained for training

sections.title: Prompt Engineering Practice: Inventory Request API
sections.body:
### Real-World Use Case
Business Context:
A small store team is building an inventory request API. Vague prompts are producing incomplete code and causing bugs in stock updates.

Developer Task:
Compare weak vs improved prompts to generate POST /requests in Express, then refine with the 4S method to include validation and status rules.

Example Input:
Weak Prompt:
"Create an inventory request endpoint."

Improved Prompt:
"In Node.js + Express + MongoDB, create POST /requests. Validate itemId exists, quantity > 0, allowed status is PENDING or APPROVED, return 201 on success and 400/404 for errors. Include Jest tests."

Expected Output:
- Endpoint with clear validation and status transitions.
- Clean controller/service separation.
- Tests for invalid item, invalid quantity, and success flow.

Why This Matters:
Better prompts reduce bugs and save developer review time.

GitHub Copilot Challenge:
Generate a first draft from a weak prompt, then iteratively improve with 4S and role prompting until code and tests pass.

exercise.title: Improve inventory endpoint generation with 4S prompts
steps: Generate endpoint with a weak prompt and note gaps.
Rewrite prompt with 4S and explicit validation rules.
Use one-shot/few-shot examples to align style.
Save final prompt template for team reuse.
expectedOutcomes: Clear quality gain from stronger prompts.
Reusable prompt template for API work.
Lower defect rate through explicit constraints.
troubleshooting: If code is generic, include exact response schema.
If tests miss edge cases, add negative examples in prompt.
If style drifts, include surrounding-file context.

quiz.question: What is GitHub Copilot?

A model powered by machine learning.
An assistant for coding, powered by OpenAI.
A service for web hosting.

It enhances the quality of code suggestions.
It automates the coding process entirely.
It implements real-time collaboration.

quiz.question: Which of the following rules is a principle of the 4S method?
options: Summarize code objectives concisely.
Specify instructions explicitly and in detail.
Streamline processes for efficient suggestions.
Simplify coding languages for universal understanding.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: How does GitHub Copilot handle personal data?
options: It saves all personal data for future references.
It shares personal data with other users.
It encrypts personal data.
It actively filters out personal data to protect privacy.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: How does Copilot use context to provide suggestions?
options: It considers only the prompt text.
It considers only file type, not content.
It considers surrounding code, file type, and open tabs.
It randomly selects context from the internet.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: Which strategy helps improve prompt effectiveness?
options: Making prompts as general as possible.
Keeping prompts lengthy and detailed.
Avoiding examples to not restrict creativity.
Providing detailed contextual information with clarity.
quiz.discussionPrompt: How would you apply this concept to your own projects?

quiz.question: What is the primary benefit of the 4S method?
options: It makes prompts longer.
It balances clarity, specificity, and conciseness for better results.
It eliminates the need for code review.
It guarantees perfect code on first try.
